lists.title: Māori A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in communicative order: pronouns, te and ngā, the sentence types he and ko, the tense-aspect particles kei te / i / ka, location with kei, negation with kāore, questions, and the a/o categories of possession.

points.title: Pronouns (au, koe, ia…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: Au (I), koe (you, one person), ia (he or she — no gender!), mātou (we, NOT including you), tātou (we, including you), koutou (you, three or more), rātou (they). Māori marks whether 'we' includes the listener — a distinction English never makes.
points.culture_note: Choosing tātou over mātou is an act of inclusion — 'Tēnā koutou, tēnā tātou katoa' welcomes everyone into the 'we'.

drills.sentence: Kei te ako {{answer}} i te reo.
Kei te haere {{answer}} ki te kura.

points.title: Te and ngā — the articles
points.function: Point to one thing or many
points.explanation:
Nouns don't change for plural — the article does the work: te whare (the house), ngā whare (the houses). This one contrast carries all of Māori number.

He (a/some) is the indefinite and gets its own point next.

points.title: He — saying what something is
points.function: Classify people and things
points.explanation: He + noun + subject makes a complete sentence with no verb 'to be': He kaiako ia (She is a teacher), He tauira au (I am a student). He is also the indefinite article — one little word, a whole sentence type.

points.title: Ko — naming and identifying
points.function: Name a specific person, place, or thing
points.explanation:
Ko identifies a SPECIFIC one: Ko Mere tēnei (This is Mere), Ko wai tō ingoa? (What is your name?). Use ko with names and definite nouns; use he to classify.

Compare: He kaiako ia (she's a teacher — one of many) vs Ko ia te kaiako (she is THE teacher).
points.culture_note: Ko opens the mihi (introduction): Ko Aoraki te maunga, ko Waitaki te awa… — naming your mountain and river before yourself.

points.title: Kei te — happening now
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: Kei te + verb marks the present: Kei te kai au (I am eating), Kei te ako mātou (we are learning). The greeting Kei te pēhea koe? (How are you?) lives on this pattern — Kei te pai (I'm good).

references.title: Te Aka Māori Dictionary: kei te

drills.hint: the first half of the present marker
the second half of the present marker

drills.sentence: Kei te {{answer}} mātou i te reo Māori.
drills.translation: We are learning the Māori language.
drills.gloss: PRESENT · ___ · we(excl) · PAST/OBJ · the · language · Māori

drills.sentence: Kei te {{answer}} au ki te toa.
drills.translation: I am going to the shop.

points.title: I — the past
points.function: Say what happened
points.explanation: I + verb marks the past: I haere au (I went), I kai mātou (we ate). The same little i also marks objects (kei te ako au i te reo) — position tells you which job it's doing.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} haere au ki te kura inanahi.
drills.gloss: ___ · go · I · to · the · school · yesterday

drills.sentence: I {{answer}} au i a ia inanahi.
drills.gloss: PAST/OBJ · ___ · I · PAST/OBJ · PERS · he/she · yesterday

points.title: Ka — what happens next
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation: Ka + verb points forward (or starts a new event in a story): Ka haere au āpōpō (I will go tomorrow), Ka kite anō tāua (see you again). Ka doesn't strictly mean future — it moves the action on — but 'will' is the right A1 instinct.
points.culture_note: Ka kite anō — 'see you again' — is the everyday goodbye.

drills.translation: We will go to the sea.

points.title: Kei — where things are
points.function: Say where someone or something is
points.explanation:
Kei + place says where something is NOW: Kei te whare ia (she is at the house), Kei Ākarana rātou (they are in Auckland). Ask with kei hea: Kei hea koe? (Where are you?).

Position words follow kei: kei runga (on), kei raro (under).

drills.translation: The books are on the table.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} raro te ngeru i te tēpu.
drills.translation: The cat is under the table.
drills.gloss: ___ · under · the · cat · PAST/OBJ · the · table

points.title: Negation with kāore
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation:
Kāore starts the negative and the subject moves forward: Kei te mōhio au → Kāore au i te mōhio (I don't know).

Past uses i: Kāore ia i haere mai (she didn't come). 'There is no…' is kāore he…: Kāore he kai (there's no food).

drills.translation: There is no food in the house.

points.title: Questions (he aha, ko wai, kei hea)
points.function: Ask about things, people, and places
points.explanation: Question words slot into the sentence types you already know: He aha tēnei? (What is this?), Ko wai tō ingoa? (Who/what is your name?), Kei hea…? (Where is…?), Nō hea koe? (Where are you from?), E hia…? (How many?).
points.culture_note: Nō hea koe? — where are you from — is often the first real question in a Māori conversation; whakapapa (origins) matter.

points.title: Possession: the a and o categories
points.function: Say whose something is — the Māori way
points.explanation:
Māori splits possession in two: Ā-category for things you control or made (tāku pukapuka — my book, tāu mahi — your work) and Ō-category for things you don't control — houses, vehicles, friends, body parts (tōku whare, tō waka, tōku hoa).

Same owner, different vowel.
points.culture_note: The a/o split encodes a worldview: your ancestors, your land, and your friends are ō — they are not possessions you command.

points.title: Statives (he nui, he pai)
points.function: Describe people and things
points.explanation: Describing words work like the he-sentence: He nui te whare (The house is big), He pai tēnei pukapuka (This book is good). No 'to be', no agreement — the stative leads and the thing described follows.

points.title: Kua — the perfect
points.function: Say something has (already) happened
points.explanation:
The particle kua marks a change of state or a completed action with present relevance — English 'has/have …': kua oti 'it's finished', kua tae mai ia 'she has arrived'. It contrasts with i (a plain past) and kei te (happening now).

Kua often pairs with kē 'already'.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} riro te tohu i a ia.

points.title: E … ana — the progressive
points.function: Say an action is/was in progress
points.explanation: E + verb + ana frames an action as ongoing, in the present or the past depending on context: e kai ana ia 'he is eating', e haere ana mātou 'we were going'. Unlike kei te (strictly now), e…ana is the neutral continuous and is what you use in narrative.

drills.sentence: E kai {{answer}} ia i tāna parāoa.
drills.gloss: prog · eat · ___ · he · at · his · bread

drills.sentence: E haere {{answer}} mātou ki te kura.
drills.gloss: prog · go · ___ · we(excl.) · to · the · school

drills.sentence: E {{answer}} ana koe i te aha?
drills.gloss: prog · ___ · prog · you · at · the · what

drills.sentence: E {{answer}} ana ahau ki a koe.

points.title: Dual & plural pronouns (māua, tātou…)
points.function: Talk about two people, and 'we' including or excluding the listener
points.explanation:
Māori pronouns distinguish singular, dual (two), and plural (three+), and for 'we' they mark whether the listener is included.

Dual: tāua (you-and-I), māua (she/he-and-I), kōrua (you two), rāua (those two).

Plural: tātou (all of us, incl. you), mātou (us, not you), koutou (you all), rātou (them).

drills.sentence: Kei te haere {{answer}} ki te toa, kōrua ko au.
drills.translation: You and I are going to the shop.

drills.sentence: Kua kai kē {{answer}}, engari kāore anō koe.
drills.translation: She and I have already eaten, but you haven't yet.
Where are you two, you and your friend?

drills.sentence: Ka haere {{answer}} katoa ki te marae.
drills.translation: All of us will go to the marae.
drills.gloss: fut · go · ___ · all · to · the · marae

drills.sentence: Nā {{answer}} tēnei mahi, ehara i a koutou.
drills.translation: This work was ours, not yours.

drills.sentence: Kei te pēhea {{answer}} e ngā tamariki?

points.title: Possessives and the a/o categories
points.function: Say 'my / your / his' with the right vowel
points.explanation:
A possessive determiner chooses a or o by the relationship, not the owner: o-category for things you don't control (parents, body, feelings, clothes, transport you ride) and a-category for things you do (children, food, tools, spouse).

Singular forms: tāku/tōku 'my', tāu/tōu 'your', tāna/tōna 'his/her'.

Plural drops the t-: āku/ōku, etc.

points.title: Passive verbs (kua kainga)
points.function: Focus on what is done, not who does it
points.explanation:
Māori uses the passive far more than English.

The verb takes a suffix — -a, -hia, -ngia, -tia, -mia and others (you learn it per verb): kai → kainga 'eaten', hanga → hangaia 'built', kite → kitea 'seen'.

The thing acted on becomes the subject, and the doer, if named, follows e: kua kainga te kai e ia 'the food has been eaten by him'.

drills.sentence: Kua {{answer}} te kai e ngā manuhiri.
drills.translation: The food has been eaten by the guests.
drills.gloss: perf · ___ · the · food · by · the(pl.) · guests

drills.translation: The house will be built by them.
The bird has been seen by me.
The song was sung by her.

drills.sentence: Kua {{answer}} te pukapuka e te kaiako.
drills.translation: The book has been read by the teacher.
drills.gloss: perf · ___ · the · book · by · the · teacher

drills.translation: You will be helped by us.

points.title: Prepositions (ki, i, hei)
points.function: Say to, from, and 'as / for the purpose of'
points.explanation: ki marks direction or goal 'to/towards' (haere ki te kura 'go to school') and the instrument 'with'. i marks the source/past location 'from/at' and the object of many verbs. hei marks a future purpose or role 'as / to be': hei aha? 'for what?', hei kaiako 'as a teacher'.

drills.sentence: Haere {{answer}} te toa, ka hoki mai.
drills.translation: Go to the shop and come back.
drills.gloss: go · ___ · the · shop · then · return · hither

drills.sentence: Nō hea koe? Nō Rotorua {{answer}} au.
drills.translation: Where are you from? I'm from Rotorua.
drills.gloss: from · where · you · from · Rotorua · ___ · I

drills.translation: This will be used as/for what?

drills.sentence: Ko ia {{answer}} rangatira mō te iwi.
drills.translation: He is (to be) a leader for the people.
drills.gloss: identify · he · ___ · chief · for · the · tribe

points.title: Nominalisation (te haerenga)
points.function: Turn an action into a noun ('the going', 'the singing')
points.explanation:
Add -nga (with linking consonants: -tanga, -hanga, -ranga) to a verb to name the action or its occasion: haere → haerenga 'trip/departure', waiata → waiatanga 'the singing', ako → akoranga 'lesson'.

The result is an ordinary noun and takes te/ngā and possessives.

drills.sentence: He rawe te {{answer}} ki te moana.
drills.translation: The trip to the sea was wonderful.
drills.gloss: stative · great · the · ___ · to · the · sea

drills.sentence: I te {{answer}} o te rā, ka hoki mātou.
drills.translation: At the setting of the sun, we returned.
drills.gloss: at · the · ___ · of · the · sun · then · return · we

drills.sentence: He roa te {{answer}} o te kapahaka.
drills.translation: The singing of the group was long.
drills.gloss: stative · long · the · ___ · of · the · performance.group

drills.sentence: Ka timata te {{answer}} ā te ata.
drills.translation: The lesson will begin in the morning.
drills.gloss: fut · begin · the · ___ · at · the · morning

drills.sentence: He nui te {{answer}} o ngā manuhiri.
drills.translation: The arrival of the guests was big.
drills.gloss: stative · big · the · ___ · of · the(pl.) · guests

drills.sentence: Kei te whakarite au i te {{answer}} kai.
drills.translation: I am preparing the making of food.
drills.gloss: prog · prepare · I · at · the · ___ · food

points.title: Nō and Mō — 'of/from' and 'for'
points.function: Say who something belongs to and who it is for
points.explanation:
nō marks origin or past belonging 'of/from' and mō marks a benefactive or future belonging 'for/about': nō wai tēnei? 'whose is this?', mō koe tēnei 'this is for you'.

Both choose their vowel by the a/o rule, giving nā/mā (a-category) beside nō/mō (o-category).

drills.translation: This is a gift for the family.

drills.sentence: He kōrero tēnei {{answer}} te tau kua pahure.
drills.translation: This is a talk about the past year.
drills.gloss: a · talk · this · ___ · the · year · perf · passed

points.title: Counting things and people
points.function: Say how many, of things and of people
points.explanation:
For plain counting, e precedes numbers two to nine: e rua ngā whare 'two houses', e whā ngā rā 'four days' (kotahi 'one' and tekau 'ten' don't take e).

For people you use the toko- prefix: tokorua 'two people', tokotoru 'three people', but tokomaha 'many'.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} rua ngā kurī i te whare.
drills.translation: There are two dogs in the house.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} toru ngā pukapuka i runga i te tēpu.
drills.translation: There are three books on the table.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} tahi anake te whare i konei.
drills.translation: There is only one house here.
drills.gloss: ___ · one · only · the · house · at · here

drills.sentence: {{answer}} whā ngā rā o te wiki nei.
drills.translation: There are four days this week.

points.title: Me — 'should' and gentle instruction
points.function: Suggest or mildly direct an action
points.explanation: me + verb gives a soft obligation or suggestion — English 'should' or 'ought to': me haere koe 'you should go', me kai tātou 'let's eat'. It's gentler than a bare command. (Note this me is different from the me that joins nouns, 'and/with'.)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} haere koe ki te moe ināianei.
drills.translation: You should go to sleep now.
drills.gloss: ___ · go · you · to · the · sleep · now

drills.sentence: {{answer}} kai tātou i mua i te haere.
{{answer}} tuhi koe i tō ingoa ki konei.
drills.translation: You should write your name here.
drills.gloss: ___ · write · you · at · your · name · to · here

drills.sentence: {{answer}} āta kōrero koe, kei rongo rātou.
drills.translation: You should speak softly, lest they hear.
We should listen to the elder.
You should drink water every day.

points.title: Kia — wishes, purpose, and 'be…!'
points.function: Express a goal, a wish, or an encouragement
points.explanation:
kia frames a state as wished-for or aimed-at rather than actual. Before a stative it gives encouragement: kia kaha 'be strong', kia tere 'be quick'.

Before a verb it marks purpose 'so that / until': tatari kia oti 'wait until it's done', ka haere au kia kite i a ia 'I'll go so as to see her'.

drills.translation: Wait until the work is done.
I will study so that I know.
Give it here so that I see.

points.title: Negating tenses (kāore i te, kīhai i)
points.function: Say something is not / was not happening
points.explanation:
Each tense negates in its own way.

Present kei te → kāore … i te: kāore au i te mōhio 'I don't know'.

Perfect kua → kāore anō … kia / kāore … i: kāore anō kia oti 'not yet finished'.

Past i → kāore … i: kāore ia i haere / kīhai ia i haere 'he didn't go'.

The verb keeps its base form.

drills.sentence: Kāore au {{answer}} te mōhio ki tērā.
drills.gloss: not · I · ___ · the · know · to · that

drills.sentence: {{answer}} rātou i haere ki te hui.
drills.translation: They did not go to the meeting.
drills.gloss: ___ · they · past · go · to · the · meeting

drills.translation: My work is not yet finished.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ia i te pai i tēnei rā.
drills.gloss: ___ · she · ___ · the · good · at · this · day

points.title: Actor emphatic — Nā … i (past) and Mā … e (future)
points.function: Highlight who did or will do an action
points.explanation:
To foreground the doer, Māori fronts the agent with nā (past) or mā (future) and marks the verb with i or e: nā Hōne i hanga te waka 'it was Hōne who built the canoe', mā wai e mahi tēnei? 'who will do this?'.

This actor-emphatic pattern is extremely common.

drills.translation: It was Hōne who built the canoe.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} wai e whakaora i te ngahere?
drills.gloss: ___ · who · fut · save · at · the · forest

drills.sentence: Mā mātou {{answer}} tiaki i ngā tamariki.
drills.translation: We will look after the children.
drills.gloss: by · us · ___ · care · at · the(pl.) · children

drills.sentence: {{answer}} rāua i waiata i te pō rā.
drills.translation: It was those two who sang that night.
drills.gloss: ___ · those.two · past · sing · at · the · night · that

drills.sentence: Mā te kaiako {{answer}} whakaako i a mātou.
drills.gloss: by · the · teacher · ___ · teach · at · (personal) · us

points.title: The causative whaka-
points.function: Turn a state or action into 'make/cause to…'
points.explanation:
The prefix whaka- makes a verb causative — 'cause to be / make happen': ako 'learn' → whakaako 'teach (make learn)', kī 'full' → whakakī 'fill', mōhio 'know' → whakamōhio 'inform', ora 'well' → whakaora 'save/heal'.

It's one of the most productive word-builders in the language.

drills.sentence: Ka {{answer}} te kaiako i te reo Māori.
drills.translation: The teacher will teach the Māori language.
drills.gloss: fut · ___ · the · teacher · at · the · language · Māori

drills.sentence: Me {{answer}} koe i te ipu ki te wai.
drills.translation: You should fill the cup with water.
drills.gloss: should · ___ · you · at · the · cup · with · the · water

drills.sentence: Kua {{answer}} au i a rātou mō te hui.
drills.translation: I have informed them about the meeting.
drills.gloss: perf · ___ · I · at · (personal) · them · for · the · meeting

drills.sentence: Nā te rongoā i {{answer}} i a ia.
drills.translation: It was the medicine that healed him.
drills.gloss: by · the · medicine · past · ___ · at · (personal) · him

drills.sentence: E {{answer}} ana ia i te whare ki te tae.
drills.translation: She is tidying the house up.
drills.gloss: prog · ___ · prog · she · at · the · house...

points.title: Relative clauses (te tangata e mōhio ana au)
points.function: Describe a noun with a whole clause
points.explanation:
Māori has no relative pronoun like 'who'.

It simply attaches a clause to the noun, usually with a resumptive or an aspect frame: te tangata e mōhio ana au 'the person (whom) I know', te pukapuka i tuhia e ia 'the book that was written by her', ngā tamariki e tākaro ana 'the children who are playing'.

drills.sentence: Ko ia te tangata {{answer}} mōhio ana au.
drills.translation: He is the person I know.
drills.gloss: identify · he · the · person · ___ · know · prog · I

drills.sentence: Anei te pukapuka {{answer}} tuhia e ia.
drills.translation: Here is the book that was written by her.
drills.gloss: here · the · book · ___ · write.pass · by · her

drills.sentence: Ngā tamariki e tākaro {{answer}} kei waho.
drills.translation: The children who are playing are outside.
drills.gloss: the(pl.) · children · prog · play · ___ · at · outside

drills.sentence: Ko te whare {{answer}} hangaia e rātou tēnā.
drills.translation: That is the house that was built by them.
drills.gloss: identify · the · house · ___ · build.pass · by · them · that

drills.sentence: Te wahine {{answer}} waiata ana he pai tōna reo.
drills.translation: The woman who is singing has a good voice.
drills.gloss: the · woman · ___ · sing · prog · stative · good · her · voice

drills.sentence: Ngā kai {{answer}} mahia e koe he reka.
drills.translation: The food that you made is delicious.
drills.gloss: the(pl.) · food · ___ · make.pass · by · you · stative · tasty

points.title: Conditions & purpose (ki te, mehemea)
points.function: Say 'if' and 'in order to'
points.explanation:
ki te + verb expresses a real condition or a purpose 'if / in order to': ki te ua, ka noho tātou 'if it rains, we'll stay', haere ki te tiki 'go in order to fetch'.

For a more hypothetical 'if', use mehemea (or ki te mea mehemea): mehemea he moni āku… 'if I had money…'.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} te ua āpōpō, ka noho tātou i te whare.
drills.translation: If it rains tomorrow, we'll stay in the house.
drills.gloss: ___ · the · rain · tomorrow · then · stay · we · at · the · house

drills.sentence: Haere {{answer}} te tiki i te wai.
drills.gloss: go · ___ · the · fetch · at · the · water

drills.sentence: {{answer}} he moni āku, ka hoko au i tērā.
drills.translation: If I had money, I would buy that.
drills.gloss: ___ · a · money · my · then · buy · I · at · that

drills.sentence: Ka reka te kai {{answer}} te tika te tunu.
drills.translation: The food is tasty if it's cooked right.
drills.gloss: then · tasty · the · food · ___ · the · correct · the · cook

drills.sentence: {{answer}} kore koe e haere, ka pōuri au.
drills.translation: If you don't go, I'll be sad.
drills.gloss: ___ · not · you · fut · go · then · sad · I

drills.sentence: {{answer}} i konei koe inanahi, kua kite koe i a ia.
drills.translation: If you had been here yesterday, you'd have seen her.

points.title: Directional particles (mai, atu, ake, iho)
points.function: Show which way an action moves
points.explanation:
Small particles after a verb show direction relative to the speaker: mai 'towards me/us' (haere mai 'come here'), atu 'away' (haere atu 'go away'), ake 'upward / on one's own', iho 'downward'.

They also colour meaning: titiro mai 'look this way', hoatu 'give (away to them)', hōmai 'give (to me)'.

drills.translation: Look over there at that mountain.

drills.sentence: Kua piki {{answer}} te rā ki te rangi.
drills.translation: The sun has risen up into the sky.
drills.gloss: perf · climb · ___ · the · sun · to · the · sky

drills.sentence: Ka heke {{answer}} te ua i te rangi.
drills.translation: The rain comes down from the sky.
drills.gloss: fut · descend · ___ · the · rain · from · the · sky

drills.sentence: Hō{{answer}} te kai ki a au.

points.title: Taea — 'can' (ka taea e au)
points.function: Say what can and can't be done
points.explanation:
Ability uses the passive verb taea with an e-agent: ka taea e au te haere 'I can go' (literally 'it will be managed by me, the going').

The thing that can be done follows as te + verb. Negate with kāore e taea: kāore e taea e ia 'she can't'.

drills.sentence: Ka {{answer}} e au te haere āpōpō.
drills.gloss: T/A · ___ · by · me · the · go · tomorrow

drills.sentence: Kāore e {{answer}} e ia te oma.
drills.gloss: not · T/A · ___ · by · him · the · run

drills.sentence: Ka taea e {{answer}} te āwhina i a koe.
drills.gloss: T/A · can · by · ___ · the · help · obj · pers · you

drills.sentence: Ka taea e rātou te {{answer}} i te waka.
drills.gloss: T/A · can · by · them · the · ___ · obj · the · car

drills.sentence: {{answer}} taea e au te kōrero Pākehā.
drills.gloss: ___ · can · by · me · the · speak · English

points.title: Kāore anō … kia — not yet
points.function: Say something hasn't happened yet
points.explanation:
The 'not yet' perfect wraps the verb in kāore anō … kia: kāore anō au kia kai 'I haven't eaten yet'.

The subject sits between anō and kia. Answering 'not yet' alone is kāore anō.

drills.sentence: Kāore anō mātou kia {{answer}} i te kai.
drills.translation: We haven't cooked the food yet.
drills.gloss: not · yet · we-excl · subj · ___ · obj · the · food

drills.sentence: Kāore anō koe {{answer}} horoi i ngā rīhi?
drills.translation: Haven't you washed the dishes yet?
drills.gloss: not · yet · you · ___ · wash · obj · the-pl · dishes

points.title: I te — the past progressive
points.function: Describe what was happening
points.explanation:
Kei te (happening now) shifts to i te for the past: i te moe ia 'she was sleeping'.

It paints an ongoing background — pair it with a point-in-time event to say what was going on when something happened.

drills.sentence: I te {{answer}} ngā tamariki i waho.
drills.gloss: past · prog · ___ · the-pl · children · at · outside

drills.sentence: I te aha koe i te {{answer}}?

points.title: Hei — purpose and 'as a'
points.function: Say what something is for
points.explanation:
hei marks purpose or future role. Before a verb: hei tapahi 'for cutting'. Before a noun: hei kaiako 'as a teacher'.

With mā-possessives it assigns the doer: he mahi hei mahi māu 'a job for YOU to do'.

drills.sentence: He aha tēnei? {{answer}} tapahi i te mīti.
drills.translation: What's this? For cutting the meat.
drills.gloss: a · what · this · ___ · cut · obj · the · meat

drills.sentence: Kua tae mai ia {{answer}} āwhina i a mātou.
drills.translation: She has come to help us.

drills.translation: This is food for you to carry.

drills.translation: Give me a knife to cut the bread.
drills.gloss: give-me · a · knife · ___ · cut · obj · the · bread

points.title: Imperatives and kaua — do and don't
points.function: Command, invite, and prohibit
points.explanation:
Short verbs take e: e tū! 'stand!', e noho! 'sit!'. Longer verbs drop it: haere mai! 'come here!'.

Prohibition is kaua e: kaua e haere! 'don't go!'. Transitive commands often use the passive: katia te kūaha! 'close the door!'.
points.culture_note: Passive imperatives (katia!, kainga!) are the polite norm for transitive commands — the bare active can sound abrupt.

drills.sentence: Kaua {{answer}} oma i roto i te whare!
drills.gloss: don't · ___ · run · at · inside · at · the · house

drills.sentence: Kaua e {{answer}} i ngā mea o ētahi atu!
drills.gloss: don't · imp · ___ · obj · the-pl · things · of · others

points.title: Ai — habitual and consequent clauses
points.function: Link habits, reasons, and 'that's when/where/why'
points.explanation:
The particle ai has no direct English equivalent: it marks a clause as habitual, consequent, or anchored to a fronted time/place/reason.

Ka oti te mahi, ka hoki ai mātou 'when the work is done, THEN we go home'. He aha koe i riri ai? 'why were you angry?'.

drills.sentence: Ka oti te mahi, ka hoki {{answer}} mātou.
drills.translation: When the work is done, then we go home.
drills.gloss: T/A · finish · the · work · T/A · return · ___ · we-excl

drills.translation: Why did you come? To learn.

points.title: Nā and Mā — ownership and agency predicates
points.function: Say whose something is and who will do what
points.explanation:
Fronted nā states realized ownership or past agency: nā Mere tēnei pukapuka 'this book is Mere's'; nā rātou i hanga 'THEY built it'.

Fronted mā is its future twin: mā wai e tunu? 'who will cook?', mā tō māmā koe e tiki 'your mum will pick you up'.

drills.translation: We two will complete this work.
Your mum will pick you up.

points.title: Ahakoa, nō te mea, engari — weaving clauses
points.function: Concede, explain, and contrast
points.explanation:
ahakoa 'although / despite': ahakoa te ua 'despite the rain'; ahakoa te aha 'no matter what'.

nō te mea / i te mea 'because'; engari 'but'. These let one sentence carry a concession, a reason, and a counterpoint.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} te ua, i haere tonu mātou.
drills.translation: Despite the rain, we still went.
drills.gloss: ___ · the · rain · past · go · still · we-excl

drills.sentence: Kāore ia i kai, {{answer}} te mea kāore ia i hiakai.
drills.translation: She didn't eat, because she wasn't hungry.

points.title: Ko-fronting — focus and identity
points.function: Put the important thing first
points.explanation:
Ko lifts a phrase to the front for identity or focus: ko Mere i haere 'it was MERE who went'.

Identity sentences pair two noun phrases: ko tēnei taku whare 'this is my house'. The fronted phrase is the answer to the question in the listener's mind.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} Mere i haere ki te hui.
drills.translation: It was Mere who went to the meeting.
drills.gloss: ___ · Mere · past · go · to · the · meeting

drills.translation: This is the very best book.
New Zealand is the beautiful land.
They were the first to arrive.

drills.sentence: Ko Hine i {{answer}} i te waiata.
drills.translation: It was Hine who composed the song.
drills.gloss: foc · Hine · past · ___ · obj · the · song

points.title: Mihi — the shape of formal greeting
points.function: Open an encounter the Māori way
points.explanation:
Formal greeting scales with the audience: tēnā koe (one person), tēnā kōrua (two), tēnā koutou (three or more), tēnā koutou katoa (everyone, all-embracing).

Welcomes pair nau mai with haere mai. Thanks reuse the greeting: tēnā koe i tō āwhina 'thank you for your help'.
points.culture_note: Mihi are not small talk — order and address matter, and a mihi acknowledges the dead, the living, and the house before business begins. These drills cover only the fixed formulae; whaikōrero (formal oratory) is a taonga to learn from kaumātua, not from an app.

drills.sentence: Tēnā {{answer}}, e ngā rangatira e rua.

points.title: Whakataukī — proverb patterns
points.function: Recognize and deploy the classic sayings
points.explanation:
Whakataukī compress values into fixed shapes. The most famous is a rhetorical question answered three times: he aha te mea nui o te ao? He tangata, he tangata, he tangata 'What is the greatest thing? It is people, people, people.'

Others are single images: he waka eke noa 'a canoe we are all in together'.
points.culture_note: Quoting a whakataukī at the right moment carries real weight — and quoting one wrongly is noticed. Sources and tribal variants matter; these six are pan-tribal classics.

drills.sentence: He aha te mea nui o te ao? He {{answer}}!
drills.translation: What is the greatest thing in the world? It is people!
drills.gloss: a · what · the · thing · big · of · the · world — a · ___
